sentences.text: My name is Henry Frankel.
Spell-- spell it?
F-R-A-N-K-E-L.
My previous name-- I was born with F-R-E-N-K-I-E-L.
The name was changed during naturalization when I came to the United States.
The only name that I was working in Warsaw for the underground was Tadek, spelled T-A-D-E-K.
I only responded to this name when I was outside of the ghetto.
There no other names that I responded to or knew that I had.
The name was given to me by the organization of the Polish Socialist Party, the PPS, spelled in Polish, [POLISH].
It's [NON-ENGLISH].
I was born June 16, 1924 in Lodz, Poland.
My father's name was Hayim Mayer, but they were calling him Max Frenkiel.
He was born in December 12, in 1888.
Always having time, interested in our school, work and participating.
Encouraging us to join Zionist organizations.
And particularly, was very fond of us when we got together on holidays and had some other people on Shabbat celebrating Shabbat in holidays in a real Jewish way.
He had the textile manufacturing place, quite a large one that he built up himself.
And he had a-- his brother was a partner to the finished goods that the plant was in [NON-ENGLISH], near Lodz. 10 kilometers north east-- north east-- northwest of Lodz.
And they distribute-- they-- a place of the business of his father, of my father, brother, and him was in Lodz.
On Narutowicza and Piortkowska Street in Lodz.
N-A-R-U-T-O-W-I-C-Z-A.
Narutowicza Street in Lodz, and Piortkowska Street, from the name of city of Piotrkow.
P-I-O-R-T-K-O-W-S-K-A Street.
There was textiles, weaving and finishing.
And a quite successful business.
I didn't really experience any hardship at home.
And my brother and my-- and me were attending private, Jewish gymnasium, predominantly of interest to father for Hebrew studies.
I attended the gymnasium from 1934 till 19-- till the outbreak of war in Poland, September 1, 1939.
My mother's name was Salomea Frenkiel, spelled F-R-E-N-K-I-E-L, but maiden name was Slomnicki, from the family of Slomnicki in Warsaw.
She originally was from Warsaw, a participant in quite a few drives for the needy children and, and a real, true mother.
Interested in developing children, and the benefit of both my, myself and my my brother and myself.
Always interested in our studies and our progress in learning.
She was very warm, an extremely loving mother with the-- knowing, with the knowledge of developing a good relation between the children-- son, the son and mother.
And always looking after people coming and having good company for us for the benefit of the developing gifts.
I also remember that from time to time, there were people from orphanages invited for Shabbats, to our house to enjoy the Shabbat meal, and staying over for going together to shul, and so on.
Mother was even participating in Warsaw when there was shortages of food and things like this.
If there was any possibility of giving and sharing with others, she always gave with the utmost.
My brother's name was Mieczyslaw, spelled M-I-E-C-Z-Y-S-L-A-W.
Mieczyslaw Frenkiel.
His Jewish name I don't remember very well, but I think it was [NON-ENGLISH].
He departed-- when he graduated from the Jewish gymnasium in Lodz, he departed for studies of textile manufacturing to, to Belgium, where he finished after the war.
The city that he departed for [NON-ENGLISH] in Belgium.
And he's living, still today, over there.
He left Poland in 1938.
He was born on January 26, 1918.
In Warsaw.
He was a very good student, extremely talented when it comes to coloring and recognizing shades in coloring.
And that's how my father decided that there should be an engineer in a family for textile industry.
During the years of war, he spent in prison or war camps.
Yes.
Whenever he didn't have to [? become, ?] or visit, or attend any business meetings, he was mostly home.
And they were like Sundays.
They were absolutely free.
We were going to soccer matches and some morning events.
There were, during the week, some concert or some Jewish entertainment, cultural events taking place in Lodz.
And we were always participating in it.
One of the-- there was a famous [NON-ENGLISH] of [NON-ENGLISH] of-- in Lodz.
And I don't remember exactly the names of the participating [INAUDIBLE], but this was one of the foremost events, cultural event of the Jewish life in Europe.
This group later became the group of Tel Aviv, theatrical group, [NON-ENGLISH].
Mm.
There was-- we observed quite well all the holidays, and there was [NON-ENGLISH] in the house and we were brought up in it.
A [NON-ENGLISH] is something that you can live with.
There's nothing wrong with it, and you can participate and enjoy it.
This, you brought it up from the home.
There was no struggle about it.
However, there too are people who will-- don't know how to deal with it and they give up before they even start.
Very, very traditional Shabbats with lighting of candles and our standing with a father who came from shul.
And standing up, and my mother lit up the candles.
We were standing in-- paying attention to it.
Yes.
Of course, there was fish and the special kugel and delicacies that we always enjoy.
Mother knew precisely which one of us liked what and this was on the table.
The funny part of it was that the children that we invited from the orphanages were always surprised how well we did remember what did they like, and was excited about.
No.
No.
There were just me and my brother.
Yes.
I have cousins of my father's brother that I was visiting.
And one of them was attending-- two of them actually, were attending the same gymnasium of-- where my brother was.
The first Jewish gymnasium in Lodz.
It was called the First Jewish Gymnasium of Lodz.
And there were two of them.
Two gymnasium of the same-- First and Second Jewish Gymnasium of Lodz.
Gymnasium means high school that you receive your matriculation.
You could continue to further studies in colleges.
I was attending the gymnasium of Itzhak Katzenelson, poet, and teacher, and educator.
I was attending first a public school, polish public school, until the age of 11, I think.
And then I was sent to the gymnasium of Itzhak Katzenelson in Lodz.
It was mixed crowd, predominantly Jewish, and also Christian.
But there was a very outstanding relation.
There was no, no bickering or, fighting, or-- [COUGHS]
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE] friends, and they were very nice, very kind.
They were many times coming to our house and we're going to their house.
And there was in case like this, there was the kibitzing of us that you can't eat anything go here because it is not kosher.
And they knew about kosher and things like this.
They knew who observed, who did not.
But the relation remained, throughout the public schools, quite good, even in the later stages of the six and seven class of the final years of public school.
The teachers were mixed.
They were Polish and Jewish.
Yeah.
Very devoted to, to the classes, and they did enjoy when they getting a response from the class of absorbing the material that they were delivering.
To gymnasium.
Yes.

Not that you switch, but if you wanted to continue, either you could go after public school to a vocational school and learn a trade, or you wanted to continue in studies, they you continued through gymnasium, and lyceum and then to university.
The gymnasium had four years of classes, including Latin, and Hebrew, and [NON-ENGLISH] and Jewish history.
And Latin was needed because it was compulsory curriculum of the Polish government.
And after the four years, you received the small matriculation papers, and you could continue to lyceum, the final two years for preparation for the colleges or universities.
And with good grades, you did not have to go through examination to enter any of the universities.
You were automatically accepted.
Even in some of the universities outside of the Poland, there were accepting people from our schools in good-- with good grades.
From the public?
Or, it's a quite great amount of learning, and we did not have the possibility of learning of algebra, geometry, trigonometry, history, geography.
These things were lumped together with-- history and geography were combined into one unit that you learned about the part of the world and there was history involved.
So it was arranged in such a way that you could absorb this material, providing that you wanted to learn.
No.
That was in gymnasium.
In the public school, there was a certain amount of pressure, but not with the same amount as you experience in probably in the gymnasium, in the high school.
Our high school were rigorous.
There were not allowances of any excuses, of headaches, and finger aches or something else.
If there was anything, you had to bring the doctor's certificate for it.
Parents did not collaborate very often.
They wanted the children to learn that there is discipline and they had to go to school to learn.
Yes.
Katzenelson was our teacher of Hebrew and of course, whenever he wrote a poem in Hebrew, he was trying to teach us to remember it.
And primarily, he was writing songs for children.
I beg your pardon?
Oh, yes.
He was, quite well.
He was number of times to, to Palestine prior to establishing the state of Israel.
And he was fluent in Hebrew, and he expected every one of his students to become fluent in Hebrew.
I developed Hebrew much later.
I-- I just was coping with my necessary demand in school.
And even though father helped us out-- father knew Hebrew and was trying to help us out.
My brother knew Hebrew much better than I, and yet, I somehow sort of went through the high school with the knowledge of Hebrew that I had.
Katzenelson went to Warsaw, and the Germans knew about him.
They were trying to corral him on several occasions without any corporation.
He was not deported during the 1942-- deportation of Jews from Warsaw.
He remained in Lodz-- he remained in Warsaw until the uprising.
I did remember seeing him sometime in 1943 in February, and then I didn't see him again.
Before the war?

Before the war, he was just a normal-- think-- any celebration in school, in the, in the gymnasium court, where they were in celebration or such thing as some holidays or thing, or event, sport event, he was always there.
And he always knew his people by his first name.
And they called his pupils by the-- students by the first name.
It brings only a good memory.
Oh, yes.
They loved him.
Whenever they have seen that Itzhak is coming, there was immediately attention paid to him.
There was a response that you don't see it quite often nowadays in schools.
Where these were the-- teachers see-- the teacher-- when the children see the teacher, they walk away.
There was no such thing with us.
We loved our teacher.
We knew who he was and what did he expect us to do.
We had four times a week, Hebrew, intensive care, intensive studies.
And--
In Hebrew. he was sometimes replaced by-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A teacher by name, Bunin, spelled B-U-N-I-N.
Whatever happened to this man, I don't know.
But I didn't see him in Warsaw when I was-- when we came to Warsaw.
He was just as good, as friendly, and outgoing individual.
Very pleasant approach with student that-- especially in the final year in gymnasium, we felt there was a certain friendship relation.
[INAUDIBLE].
There was not-- that's just like teaching someone something, but he would sit down with us and try to incite us to speak in Hebrew to him, and he would respond slowly so we can absorb it with great--
Bunin and, and Katzenelson.
Extremely friendly people.
And I was-- it was very fun talking about them.
Well, they had-- quite a large school for the size of the building that occupy.
The building is still standing today in Lodz.
It was, of course, confiscated by the Polish government and turned into a medical school.
No.
We were living-- I had to take a tram and then, and another tram.
So there was something like, 20 blocks away.
We were living on Lealee Koszcuiczo, eight.
And the other place was in [NON-ENGLISH]
From 8:00 to 4:00 in the afternoon and sometimes till 5:00 afternoon.
Saturdays were off, but we had school on Sunday.
I beg your pardon?
No.
There were Hebrew, and Polish, and Latin was compulsory.
We-- not until I graduated from this, what they called, small high school, the small gymnasium.
And all I had to do is get two more years of lyceum for preparation of-- for the high studies in colleges.
The war broke out in 1939, and I couldn't continue.
There were no possibilities of anybody to study in any high school or anything like it.
Oh, yes.
Yeah.
There was quite a number of incidents in Poland and Lodz, despite the fact that the quite large percentage of Jews were living in Lodz.
I'd like to stress the fact that Lodz had something slightly over 600,000 inhabitants, and about 49% were Jewish.
So this was quite a large community of Jewish population.
And yet, there were anti-Semitic outbreaks in some parts of Lodz.
No.
I-- not personally.
I was always surrounded with friends of Jewish faith, and I could not say that I experienced it.
But I know quite a bit, quite a few things.
And you read in the papers, in some small towns, how badly things were mistreated.
There was quite a famous priest, by name Trzecziak, who was just getting simply, absolutely, mad when he could spot a Jew in his vicinity or in place near him.
And he incited people to conduct himself in very unhuman manner.
I met Trzecziak in Warsaw by accident when I was-- when I entered the Cathedral of the Three Crosses.
There was a different man then.
He was very polite.
He-- when I walked in and he knew that I was Jewish, and he received me very well.
This was in 1941, in winter of 1941.
Oh, Very, very hard name to spell.
T-R-Z-E-C-Z-I-A-K.
Trzecziak.
No, no.
This was accidental need for stepping inside and receiving some warm water because of the severe cold winter in 1941.
And I received the cold water and some piece of bread with some tea and, then I left without being-- becoming a nuisance or asking for more.
Mm.
Actually, prior to the outbreak of World War II, there were many Jewish people from Germany shipped to Poland, especially the one that had built a connection with Poland.
And they were expelled from Germany for being Polish subjects.
And whatever they were experiencing, that somehow people could not believe that this thing can happen.
And there was done in such a brutal manner.
They just asked them to pack up whatever they could, and take it with them, and leave.
Even the people that really know that this is true, whatever they went through, they did not respond to it with full faith until they was too late.
Disappointing that a country like-- described by so many people of culture, and especially music, and poetry, and so on, and so one, could come up with-- could deliver such unusual blows to humanity.
Well, we were reading and we have seen in the movies, on the short news, what was going on in Germany, this Kristallnacht and the other things.
And we were absolutely upset and-- but still, there was certain security that we did not want to accept that this is the truth.
And of course, later on, things happened.
